channel.title: Make Money Online on TechLife Guide
channel.description: Recent content in Make Money Online on TechLife Guide

item.title: Best Side Hustles You Can Start Today (No Experience)
item.description: Discover 7 high-impact side hustles you can launch in 24 hours. Real examples, actionable steps, and zero fluff. Start earning cash now.
item.content:encoded:
TITLE: Best Side Hustles You Can Start Today (No Experience) DESCRIPTION: Discover 7 high-impact side hustles you can launch in 24 hours. Real examples, actionable steps, and zero fluff. Start earning cash now. TAGS: side hustles, make money online, freelance work, passive income, lifestyle tips

Introduction

I remember staring at my bank account at 11 PM on a Tuesday, realizing that my full-time salary wasn’t cutting it anymore. Not because I was reckless, but because life got expensive. That night, I started researching side hustles. Not the vague “sell your old clothes” advice, but real, repeatable ways to generate cash flow within days.

Fast forward three years, and I’ve tested over a dozen side hustles. Some flopped. Some turned into full-time incomes. In this post, I’m sharing the ones that actually work—the ones you can start today without a degree, a portfolio, or a big upfront investment.

Why Most Side Hustle Advice Fails

Before we dive in, let’s address the elephant in the room. Most side hustle articles are recycled lists of “become a dog walker” or “take online surveys.” Those aren’t bad, but they rarely produce meaningful income. The real problem is that people treat side hustles like hobbies. They spend weeks planning, buying equipment, or building a website. That’s not a side hustle—that’s a second job that hasn’t started yet.

The best side hustles have three things in common:

  • Low barrier to entry (you can start in hours, not months)
  • Immediate cash flow (you get paid within days or weeks)
  • Scalable effort (more work = more money, no ceiling)

Let’s look at seven that meet all three criteria.

1. Freelance Micro-Tasks on Upwork or Fiverr

You don’t need to be a graphic designer or a software engineer. The most underrated side hustle is doing small, boring tasks that other people hate.

What You Can Do

  • Data entry (copying text from PDFs to spreadsheets)
  • Virtual assistance (scheduling emails, organizing inboxes)
  • Transcription (turning audio into text)
  • Proofreading (catching typos in blog posts)

Real Example

My friend Sarah started on Upwork doing “Excel cleanup” for $15 per hour. She had zero experience—just basic spreadsheet knowledge. Her first job was organizing a real estate agent’s client list. Within three months, she was earning $800 per month working 10 hours a week.

Actionable Tip

Create a profile today. For your first job, bid low ($10–$15 per hour) and over-deliver. Ask for a review. That review unlocks higher-paying gigs. Most freelancers fail because they overprice before building a track record.

2. Print-on-Demand (Zero Inventory)

Print-on-demand (POD) lets you sell custom designs on t-shirts, mugs, phone cases, and tote bags without buying any inventory. A third-party company prints and ships the product when someone orders.

How to Start

  1. Choose a platform: Printful, Redbubble, or Teespring.
  2. Design simple graphics using Canva (free) or hire a designer on Fiverr for $20.
  3. Upload designs to your store.
  4. Promote on social media or Pinterest.

Real Example

My neighbor’s teenage son created a line of sarcastic cat quotes on Redbubble. He spent two hours designing ten shirts. Six months later, he’s earning $200–$400 per month from passive sales. He hasn’t touched the store in weeks.

Actionable Tip

Don’t try to appeal to everyone. Pick a niche: “dog owners who hate mornings” or “teachers who love coffee.” Niche products sell because they feel personal. Generic designs get buried.

3. Renting Out Your Car or Parking Spot

If you own a car that sits in your driveway most of the day, it’s a wasted asset. Apps like Turo (for car rental) and Neighbor (for parking space rental) let you monetize that empty space.

How It Works

  • Turo: List your car, set availability, and earn money when someone rents it. You set the price, and Turo provides insurance.
  • Neighbor: Rent out your driveway, garage, or even a spare parking spot for monthly storage.

Real Example

A coworker rents his 2018 Honda Civic on Turo for $40 per day. He earns about $600 per month. The car is paid off, so that’s pure profit minus maintenance. He only rents it out when he’s working from home.

Actionable Tip

Start with your personal vehicle. Take good photos, clean the interior, and set a competitive price (check similar cars in your area). Limit availability to weekends first to test demand.

4. Selling Digital Products on Etsy

Physical products require shipping, returns, and inventory management. Digital products—like planners, resume templates, or social media graphics—require one-time effort and infinite sales.

What Sells Well

  • Printable planners (meal planners, budget trackers)
  • Resume and cover letter templates
  • Canva templates for Instagram posts
  • Digital art prints (wall art, quotes)

Real Example

A college student I know created a “digital budget planner” in Canva and listed it on Etsy for $7. She sold 30 copies in the first month. That’s $210 for two hours of work. She now has 15 products earning her $1,200 per month.

Actionable Tip

Browse Etsy for “digital planner” or “resume template.” Look for products with high reviews but low competition. Create a similar product with a unique angle (e.g., “Budget Planner for Freelancers” instead of generic “Budget Planner”).

5. Local Service Hustles (High Demand, Low Competition)

Most people overlook local services because they think they need special skills. But there’s massive demand for simple, physical tasks that people hate doing.

Ideas

  • Gutter cleaning: Homeowners dread it. Charge $100–$200 per house. No experience needed.
  • Pressure washing: Rent a machine for $40/day, charge $150 per driveway.
  • Pet waste removal: Yes, scooping poop. Charge $15 per visit. Clients pay monthly.
  • Christmas light installation: Seasonal, but huge profit margins.

Real Example

My cousin started a “poop scooping” business in his neighborhood. He printed 200 flyers (cost: $20) and dropped them in mailboxes. He got 12 clients in the first week. He spends 30 minutes per client, twice a week, earning $360 per week. Total startup cost: $20 for gloves, bags, and flyers.

Actionable Tip

Pick one service and test it on Nextdoor or Facebook Marketplace. Offer a discount for first-time customers. Word-of-mouth is your best marketing tool.

6. AI-Assisted Content Creation

You don’t need to be a writer or a designer. AI tools like ChatGPT, Canva AI, and Midjourney let you produce content that people will pay for.

Ways to Monetize

  • Write short blog posts for small businesses (use ChatGPT for drafts, then edit)
  • Create social media captions for local restaurants or gyms
  • Generate AI art for book covers or wall art (sell on Etsy)
  • Edit AI-generated videos using CapCut or InShot

Real Example

A retiree in my online group uses ChatGPT to write “weekly tips” newsletters for a real estate agent. He charges $150 per month for four emails. The work takes two hours total. He has three clients, earning $450 per month.

Actionable Tip

Offer a free sample. Reach out to a local business on Instagram and say, “I’ll write your next three social media posts for free. If you like them, hire me for $100/month.” Most will say yes.

7. Mystery Shopping and Product Testing

Companies pay regular people to test their products and services. You get paid to eat at restaurants, shop at stores, or try new apps.

Legitimate Platforms

  • UserTesting: Get paid $10 for 20-minute website reviews (record your screen and voice)
  • Mystery Shopper: Sign up with companies like Market Force or Secret Shopper
  • Product testing: Amazon Vine, Influenster, or PINCHme (free products + occasional cash)

Real Example

I signed up for UserTesting last year. In the first week, I completed five tests and earned $50. Each test took about 15 minutes. It’s not life-changing money, but it’s easy and requires zero skill.

Actionable Tip

Avoid any site that asks for an upfront fee. Legitimate mystery shopping companies never charge you to join.

How to Choose the Right Side Hustle for You

Not every side hustle fits every person. Here’s how to pick:

  • If you have 30 minutes per day: Try UserTesting or micro-tasks on Fiverr.
  • If you have 5 hours per week: Start print-on-demand or digital products.
  • If you want passive income: Digital products or renting out assets.
  • If you want fast cash: Local services (gutter cleaning, pet waste removal).
  • If you hate people: Freelance writing or AI content creation.

Start with one. Not two. Not three. Pick the one that feels easiest and do it for 30 days. Most people fail because they try to do everything at once.

Final Thoughts

The best side hustle isn’t the one that makes the most money—it’s the one you actually start. I’ve seen people earn $50,000 per year from a side hustle they started on a whim. I’ve also seen people buy courses, watch YouTube videos, and never take action.

You don’t need a perfect plan. You need a single step. Pick one idea from this list, set up your profile or listing today, and send your first offer or post your first product tonight. The money will follow.

Your bank account doesn’t care about your excuses. It cares about your next move.

item.title: Make Money Online as a Student: 7 Legit Side Hustles That Work
item.description: Tired of being broke? Learn 7 realistic ways to make money online as a student without scams or BS. Actionable tips from someone who did it.
item.content:encoded:
TITLE: Make Money Online as a Student: 7 Legit Side Hustles That Work DESCRIPTION: Tired of being broke? Learn 7 realistic ways to make money online as a student without scams or BS. Actionable tips from someone who did it. TAGS: student side hustle, make money online, student income, online jobs for students, passive income for students

Introduction

I remember sitting in a campus coffee shop, staring at my bank account on my phone. $12.47. That was supposed to last me two weeks. I had a part-time job at the library, but after rent and textbooks, there was nothing left for actual life—no pizza with friends, no new jeans, no buffer for emergencies.

Sound familiar?

The good news is that you don’t need a trust fund or a second retail job to fix this. The bad news is that 90% of the “make money online” content out there is either a scam or useless fluff. “Just start a blog!” Great, but that takes six months to see a dollar. “Sell stuff on Amazon!” Cool, but you need capital most students don’t have.

I spent two years testing what actually works for students with limited time, zero startup cash, and no special skills. Below are seven methods I used personally—or watched friends use—to earn real money while keeping grades up.

Why Most Student Side Hustles Fail

Before we dive into the list, let’s kill a myth. You don’t need to be a coding genius, a graphic design prodigy, or a TikTok influencer to make money online. What you do need is a system that respects your schedule.

Most students fail because they pick a hustle that demands consistency they can’t give. Freelance writing is great, but if you have midterms and can’t deliver a draft, you lose the client. Dropshipping sounds easy, but customer service during finals week is a nightmare.

The methods below work because they fit around your life—not the other way around.

1. Sell Your Class Notes and Study Guides

This is the lowest-hanging fruit. You’re already studying. Why not get paid for it?

Platforms like StudySoup, Stuvia, and Nexus Notes let you upload your notes, flashcards, and study guides. Other students pay to download them. The key is to make your notes clear, organized, and visually easy to scan. Don’t just scan your messy handwriting—type them up, use bullet points, and highlight key terms.

Real example: A friend of mine at a large state university uploaded her biology notes from a notoriously hard professor. She made $1,200 in one semester. Not bad for work she was already doing.

Actionable tip: Focus on high-demand courses: STEM, accounting, economics, and nursing. Also, check if your school has a specific note-sharing program. Some universities have internal marketplaces.

How much you can make: $50–$300 per course per semester. If you take 4–5 classes, that’s real money.

2. Become a “Micro-Tasker” for AI Training Data

This sounds technical, but it’s not. Companies training AI models need humans to do small, repetitive tasks: label images, transcribe short audio clips, rate search results, or write short sentences.

Platforms like Appen, Clickworker, and Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk) are the main players. The pay is modest—usually $8–$15 per hour—but the work is dead simple and you can do it between classes.

Why this works for students: Tasks take 2–10 minutes. You can do five during a 15-minute break between lectures. No long-term commitment. No client management.

Real example: I used Clickworker during my sophomore year. I’d spend 20 minutes a day labeling photos of street signs. I made about $120 a month. Not life-changing, but it covered my Spotify, Netflix, and coffee habit.

Actionable tip: Sign up for multiple platforms at once. Work can be inconsistent, so having three options ensures steady tasks.

How much you can make: $100–$400 per month, depending on hours.

3. Tutoring (But Not How You Think)

Traditional tutoring—sitting across from a struggling freshman—pays okay but eats up time. Online tutoring is better, but even that usually means rigid scheduling.

The smarter play is asynchronous tutoring. Platforms like Chegg Tutors, Course Hero, and Wyzant let you answer student questions on your own time. Someone posts a calculus problem at 2 AM? You answer it when you wake up. No video calls. No scheduling.

Why this works: You can answer questions during your natural downtime—waiting for laundry, riding the bus, or procrastinating on a paper.

Actionable tip: Stick to subjects you actually aced. If you got an A in intro psychology, you can answer those questions in minutes. Don’t try to tutor in organic chemistry if you barely passed.

How much you can make: $15–$30 per hour of actual work. But because you’re answering questions in bursts, you can easily earn $200–$500 a month.

4. User Testing Websites and Apps

Companies pay people to test their websites and apps before launch. You record your screen and voice, saying what you think as you click around. It’s weird at first, but it’s easy money.

Platforms: UserTesting, Userlytics, and TryMyUI.

Each test pays $10 for about 20 minutes of work. The catch is that you don’t get tests every day. But when you do, it’s a quick $10.

Why this works for students: You can do a test during a lunch break. No commitment. No ongoing relationship. Just show up, talk, get paid.

Real example: A friend in my dorm did UserTesting for a year. He averaged 8–10 tests per month. That’s $80–$100 for roughly three hours of work total.

Actionable tip: Be honest. Don’t try to sound like a professional UX designer. They want natural reactions. Say “This button confuses me” or “I have no idea what to click next.” That’s gold to them.

How much you can make: $50–$150 per month, depending on how many tests you qualify for.

5. Flip Free Items (Zero Startup Cost)

You don’t need money to start flipping. You need a bike or a willingness to walk.

The play is simple: Find free items on Facebook Marketplace, Craigslist, or the “Free” section of your local classifieds, clean them up, and resell them.

I did this with furniture. Students moving out of dorms often leave behind decent desks, chairs, and lamps because they can’t take them. I’d pick them up, wipe them down, and list them on Marketplace for $20–$50. My best flip was a solid oak desk I found for free. I sanded it (30 minutes of work), stained it ($8 for stain), and sold it for $150.

Why this works: Students are constantly moving in and out. There’s a never-ending supply of free or cheap furniture. Plus, you don’t need a car—a hand truck or a friend with a hatchback works.

Actionable tip: Focus on items that are annoying to move: bookshelves, desks, mattresses (if clean), and small appliances. People pay a premium for convenience.

How much you can make: $100–$500 per month, depending on how much time you spend hunting.

6. Sell Digital Products (Not E-books)

Skip the “write an e-book” advice. That takes weeks. Instead, sell small, useful digital products that take a few hours to create.

What sells well for students:

  • Study templates (Notion templates for semester planning, Google Docs essay templates)
  • Resume templates (specifically for college students applying for internships)
  • Budget spreadsheets (for other students trying to save money)
  • Planner printables (weekly, monthly, habit trackers)

Sell them on Etsy, Gumroad, or Ko-fi. Price them at $3–$10. You don’t need thousands of sales—50 sales at $5 each is $250.

Real example: A student I know made a “College Budget Spreadsheet” in an afternoon. She listed it on Etsy for $4. Over two years, she sold over 1,200 copies. That’s nearly $5,000 from one afternoon of work.

Actionable tip: Look at what’s trending on Etsy in the “Digital Products” category. Replicate the format but add your own twist. Don’t copy—just get inspired.

How much you can make: $50–$500 per month, growing over time as your products accumulate sales.

7. Short-Term Rentals of Your Dorm Room (Yes, Really)

This one is niche but powerful. If you live off-campus or in a dorm with a private room, you can rent it out during breaks when you go home.

How it works: During winter break, spring break, or summer, list your room on SpareRoom or Airbnb (if allowed by your lease/dorm rules). Travelers, interns, or other students needing short-term housing will pay for a furnished room.

Real example: A friend in a college town rented her room for $35/night during spring break. She went home for 10 days and made $350. The tenant was a graduate student visiting for research.

Warning: Check your lease and school policies. Some dorms forbid subletting. Don’t risk eviction.

Actionable tip: If you can’t sublet your room, offer to housesit or pet-sit for professors or neighbors who travel during breaks. Apps like Rover (for pets) or TrustedHousesitters work well.

How much you can make: $100–$500 per break.

Putting It All Together: How to Start This Week

You don’t need to do all seven. Pick two that fit your schedule and personality.

  • If you’re already organized and take good notes: Start with selling notes (Method 1) and micro-tasks (Method 2).
  • If you’re good at explaining things: Try tutoring (Method 3) and user testing (Method 4).
  • If you like hunting for deals: Go with flipping (Method 5).
  • If you prefer creating once and selling forever: Build digital products (Method 6).

One hard rule: Never pay money upfront for a “job opportunity.” If someone asks you to pay for a “starter kit” or “registration fee,” it’s a scam. Every method above is free to start.

Final thought: The goal isn’t to get rich overnight. It’s to build a small income stream that gives you breathing room—so you can say yes to a pizza night, buy a textbook without panic, or save for a trip after graduation. Start with one method this week. See how it feels. Then adjust.

You’ve got the time. You’ve got the skills. Now go make that $12.47 look like pocket change.

item.title: Smart Tech Side Hustles to Boost Your Income in 2025
item.description: Discover profitable tech side hustle ideas with real examples and actionable steps. From freelancing to digital products, start earning today.
item.content:encoded:
TITLE: Smart Tech Side Hustles to Boost Your Income in 2025 DESCRIPTION: Discover profitable tech side hustle ideas with real examples and actionable steps. From freelancing to digital products, start earning today. TAGS: tech side hustles, make money online, freelance tech work, passive income tech, lifestyle

Introduction

Last year, I watched a friend quit his corporate marketing job after his side hustle—building simple automation scripts for local real estate agents—started paying his rent twice over. He didn’t have a computer science degree. He just saw a boring, repetitive task and figured out how to kill it with a few lines of code.

That’s the beauty of tech side hustles today. You don’t need to be a Silicon Valley engineer. You just need a specific problem to solve and a willingness to learn one tool deeply. Whether you’re looking to pay off debt, save for a trip, or eventually replace your full-time income, the tech space offers more leverage than almost any other industry. A single hour of work can generate value for hundreds of people. A single digital product can sell while you sleep.

But here’s the catch: most people waste months trying to learn everything before they start. They buy courses on Python, web development, and blockchain all at once. They never ship anything. This post is the opposite. I’m going to give you five specific, proven tech side hustles you can start this week. Each one includes a real example, a clear first step, and the exact tools you’ll need.

Let’s cut the noise.

Why Tech Side Hustles Are Different

Most side hustles trade time for money. You wash a car, you get paid. You walk a dog, you get paid. That’s fine, but it caps your earning potential at your physical availability.

Tech side hustles break that ceiling. A single automation script can save a client 20 hours a month. A well-designed Notion template can be sold to 1,000 people. A freelance website project can lead to a retainer that pays you for three hours of maintenance each month. The work scales, and your skills compound. The more you build, the faster you get. The faster you get, the more you can charge.

Plus, the demand is real. Small businesses, solopreneurs, and even large corporations are desperate for people who can make technology work without a 200-page manual. They don’t need a full-time hire. They need someone who can fix a broken email workflow, build a simple landing page, or clean up a messy spreadsheet. That someone can be you.

1. No-Code Automation Specialist

What It Is

You help businesses automate repetitive tasks using tools like Zapier, Make (formerly Integromat), or n8n. Think of it as digital plumbing. A real estate agent wants new leads from their Facebook ad to automatically go into a Google Sheet and trigger a text message. A coach wants their Calendly bookings to create a Trello card and send a welcome email. You build those connections.

Real Example

Sarah, a former teacher, started offering “automation audits” on Upwork. She would spend 30 minutes looking at a small business owner’s workflow, then propose three automations that would save them time. Her first client was a wedding photographer who spent two hours each week manually sending invoice reminders. Sarah built a Zap that did it in zero seconds. She charged $150. The photographer was thrilled. Within six months, Sarah had a waitlist and was charging $500 per project.

Actionable Tips

  • Start with one platform. Master Zapier first. It’s the most popular and has the most integrations.
  • Find your niche. Don’t try to automate everything. Focus on real estate agents, coaches, or e-commerce stores. Learn their specific pain points.
  • Offer a “free audit.” Go on LinkedIn or Facebook groups for small business owners. Offer to look at one of their workflows for free. If you find an automation opportunity, show them the value. Then quote a price.
  • Tools to learn: Zapier, Make, Google Sheets, Airtable.

2. Selling Digital Templates and Notion Setups

What It Is

You design and sell pre-built templates for productivity tools like Notion, Google Sheets, or Canva. People will pay for a system that saves them setup time. A student might buy a Notion dashboard for tracking assignments. A freelancer might pay for an invoice tracker in Google Sheets. A content creator might want a Canva template pack for Instagram stories.

Real Example

Jake was a college student who built a Notion template to track his job applications. He shared it on Reddit, and 200 people asked him for a copy. He cleaned it up, added a tutorial video, and listed it on Gumroad for $19. That week, he made $3,800. He now runs a small store with 15 templates and earns a consistent $2,000 per month in passive income.

Actionable Tips

  • Solve a specific problem. Don’t build a “general productivity template.” Build a “meal planning template for busy moms” or a “content calendar for solo podcasters.”
  • Use Gumroad or Etsy. Gumroad takes a small cut but handles delivery and payments. Etsy has built-in traffic.
  • Make it beautiful but functional. People buy with their eyes first. Spend time on layout and color. Use a clean font.
  • Include a video walkthrough. This reduces refund requests and increases perceived value.
  • Tools to learn: Notion, Google Sheets, Canva, Figma (for design).

3. Freelance Website Maintenance

What It Is

Many small businesses have a website but no one to update it. They need plugin updates, security checks, broken link fixes, or simple content changes like swapping a photo or updating a price. They don’t want to hire an agency. They want a reliable person they can text.

Real Example

Carlos started offering website maintenance to local restaurants. He charged $75 per month for a “basic care” package: weekly backups, plugin updates, and one hour of content changes. He found his first client by walking into a pizza shop that had a broken “order online” button. He fixed it on the spot for free. The owner hired him that week. Carlos now manages 30 sites and earns $2,250 per month in recurring revenue.

Actionable Tips

  • Start with WordPress. It powers over 40% of the web. You only need to know how to update plugins, change text, and manage backups.
  • Offer a free “health check.” Use a tool like GTmetrix or Pingdom to show a business owner their site speed score. Offer to fix one issue for free. Then pitch your monthly plan.
  • Create three tiers. Basic ($50-75/mo for updates and backups), Standard ($100-150/mo plus monthly content changes), Premium ($200-300/mo with SEO tweaks and analytics reports).
  • Tools to learn: WordPress, Elementor (page builder), UpdraftPlus (backups), Google Search Console.

4. AI Prompt Engineering for Small Teams

What It Is

You help people use AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, or Midjourney effectively. Most people type a bad prompt, get a bad result, and give up. You learn how to craft prompts that produce useful, accurate outputs. Then you sell that skill as a service or a product.

Real Example

Mia, a freelance writer, noticed that her clients were struggling to use ChatGPT for social media captions. She created a “Prompt Pack” with 50 tested prompts for Instagram, LinkedIn, and Twitter. She sold it for $27 on Gumroad. She also offered a one-hour consulting session where she taught a small business owner how to use AI for their email newsletters. She charges $150 per session.

Actionable Tips

  • Build a library of prompts. Start with one niche (e.g., real estate listings, fitness content, or lesson plans for teachers). Test each prompt until it consistently produces good results.
  • Sell prompt packs or templates. Use Gumroad or Payhip. Include instructions on how to customize them.
  • Offer “AI onboarding” to small teams. Many small businesses have purchased ChatGPT subscriptions but don’t use them. You can show them how to automate customer service responses, draft blog posts, or summarize meeting notes.
  • Tools to learn: ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, Midjourney.

5. Low-Code App Building with Glide or Bubble

What It Is

You build simple mobile apps or web apps without writing traditional code. Platforms like Glide (for internal tools and simple apps) and Bubble (for more complex web apps) let you drag and drop components. You can build a client portal, a booking app, or a habit tracker.

Real Example

Tom, a project manager, built a simple app for a local gym to track member check-ins and class bookings using Glide. The gym had been using paper sign-in sheets. Tom’s app replaced that in two days. He charged $800 for the build and $50 per month for hosting and support. He now has 12 similar clients, all local service businesses.

Actionable Tips

  • Focus on internal tools. Businesses need apps for inventory tracking, employee scheduling, or customer management. These don’t need to be in the App Store.
  • Learn Glide first. It’s easier than Bubble and perfect for data-driven apps that connect to Google Sheets.
  • Find a client before you build. Ask local businesses, “What’s one process you wish you could do on your phone?” If they have a clear answer, you have a project.
  • Tools to learn: Glide, Bubble, Airtable, Google Sheets.

How to Choose the Right Hustle for You

You don’t need to try all five. In fact, that’s a recipe for burnout. Here’s a simple framework:

  • If you love organizing and efficiency: Go with automation specialist or digital templates.
  • If you enjoy fixing things and helping people: Go with website maintenance.
  • If you’re curious about AI and writing: Go with prompt engineering.
  • If you like building visual products: Go with low-code app building.

Pick one. Dedicate two weeks to learning the basics and landing your first client or making your first sale. Don’t worry about perfection. Your first Zapier automation will have a bug. Your first Notion template will be ugly. That’s fine. Ship it, get feedback, improve.

The First Step (Do This Today)

Stop reading. Open a new tab. Go to Gumroad or Etsy and search for “Notion template” or “Zapier automation.” Look at what’s selling. Look at the reviews. What are people complaining about? What’s missing? That’s your idea.

Or, open LinkedIn and search for “small business owner” in your city. Send three of them a message: “I’m starting a side hustle helping small businesses save time with simple tech. Would you be open to a free 15-minute audit of one of your workflows?”

You’ll get ignored by two. One will say yes. That one is your start.

The tech side hustle landscape is wide open. The barrier to entry has never been lower. The only thing stopping you is the belief that you need to be an expert before you start. You don’t. You just need to be one step ahead of the person you’re helping.

Go be that step.
